questions.question: Which access control strategy is the strongest in Windchill, overriding all other rules?
questions.rationale: Security labels are the strongest rules in Windchill's access control scheme. They deny all access to non-authorized participants and override any other access control mechanism, including domain policies and ad hoc permissions.

questions.question: What happens when no access control rule is set for a participant on an object?
options: The participant is granted Read access by default
The participant is denied access by default
The participant is granted Full Control
The system prompts the administrator to create a rule
questions.rationale: If no rule is set for a participant, access is denied by default. This is the weakest level in the priority hierarchy - the 'default deny' that applies when no other rule matches.

questions.question: Where should a business administrator focus most domain administration efforts?
questions.rationale: Business administrators should concentrate on data domains (Default, PDM, Project) which govern access, notification, and indexing rules for data objects. User, System, and Site domains have essential defaults that should not be modified.

questions.question: Which of the following can ad hoc permissions do?
options: Grant and deny access to individual objects
Only grant access to individual objects
Only deny access to all objects of a type
Override security labels on individual objects
questions.rationale: Ad hoc permissions can only grant access - they cannot deny it. They apply to individual object instances and supersede domain policy deny rules, but cannot override security labels or absolute deny permissions.
